entry.title: S/4 HANA Extensibility – Guide to extend API_BUSINESS_PARTNER API for Standard Fields
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community Members,

We hope this blog finds you well.

In this, I will explain how to identify and extend API_BUSINESS_PARTNER API for Standard fields.

Business Scenario:

API_BUSINESS_PARTNER is a standard API for creating/updating the Business Partner in S/4 HANA.

But few standard Business Partner fields are missing as part of the API.

In this blog, will extend KNB1-LOCKB (Lock Box) field. Similarly other standard fields can be extended.

Finding the Service Definition for the Standard API

The first step is to locate the CDS Data Definitions. For our example, the CDS Data Definition is A_BusinessPartner. You can find the CDS Data Definitions using the sql view name or alternatively, you can use Eclipse to perform a pattern search with the CDS Data Definition name.

Once you have the CDS Data Definition, identify the fields within the entities present in the data definition. For our requirement, we needed to add the lockb field. The path for this field is as follows:

  • A_BusinessPartner
    •  A_Customer
      • A_CustomerCompany

Creating Extend View

Now extend the CDS view (A_CustomerCompany) using Eclipse. This is done by creating a new “Data Definition” element and the “Extend View” template.

  1.  Launch Eclipse and connect to your system.
  2.  Click File -> New -> Other -> Data Definition
  3.  Enter Name and Description, click next and select transport.
  4.  Select ‘Extend View’ Template.
  5.  Add the additional fields lockb from another I_CustomerCompany CDS view.
  6.  Activate your extension

Important Notes:

Make sure view field name(lockb) is same as table field name(knb1-lockb). So that the data mapping is done automatically.

Conclusion

You have successfully extended the standard API. The newly added field can now be seen in the standard API response.

entry.title: IBP RTI - Cloud Connector high availability setup
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog is to write about the Idea to consume the high availability solution of SAP Cloud connector, where SAP ABAP application is calling the cloud services using RFC(Om-Premise to Cloud connectivity) using cloud connector.

With SAP cloud connector we have the high availability setup(Master - Shadow instances), which is more than enough for cloud to on-premise connections to have seamless connectivity, wherein calling the cloud services from on-premise we need additional setup to utilize the high availability setup, like some short of cluster or load balancers to identify and redirect the traffic always to master node.

Here we will discuss on using the Azure TCP load balancer between the SAP ABAP and SAP Cloud Connector and use TCP load balancer IP/FQDN in the SAP RFC connection.

Analysis based on various SAP Note, SAP community blogs and Microsoft learning blogs.

Pre-requisite

  1. SAP cloud connector 2.15.0 (Master Role Check monitoring API) required as Master role check monitoring API is available with version 2.15.0 onwards. 
  2. Azure TCP load balancer -> For traffic routing to master node based on the results of calling the Master Role Check monitoring API for the SAP cloud connector Nodes available in the backend pool using the health probes. 

Custom HTTP/HTTPS health probe for Azure Load Balancer will be required to translate the result(true) of Master Role check API to http status code 200 for marking the master node as healthy to route all the traffic to master node.

Solution

 

 

  1. Configure SAP Cloud Connector instance in high availability setup:
    • Set up two SAP Cloud Connector instances: a Master instance and a Shadow instance, both should be configured.
    • sapcc1 as master and sapcc2 as shadow instance 

    •  

       

    • Ensure both instances are registered and operational, with the Shadow instance ready to take over if the Master becomes unavailable. 
    • Configure a service channel to be called by ABAP client RFC.

       

      Target host used in the RFC will be the IP/FQDN of the TCP load balancer deployed in below steps. For our demo we used the instance as 85 on SAP RFC i.e. 3385 as service channel port

  2. Deploy a TCP Load Balancer in Azure: 
    • Create a Standard TCP Load Balancer in Azure, which can distribute traffic based on the TCP connections to either the Master or the Shadow SCC instance. 
    • Assign a Frontend IP: Choose whether to use a public or private IP, depending on whether the load balancer needs to be accessible internally or externally. 
    • Set up a Backend Pool: Add both the Master and Shadow SCC instances (or the respective VMs hosting these instances) to the backend pool.

       

  3. Set Up Health Probes for Failover:  
    • Create a HTTP health probe for monitoring the master SCC instances. Specify the custom URI/API to determine the master SCC instance (refer to custom health probe creation/configuration to consume the Master role check API available in SCC). Here we are using the custom API(Python API to consume SCC master role check API) hosted as service on the SCC VM's.

       

      Python sample code used and deployed as service and configured to start automatically-

from flask import Flask, jsonify
import requests
import logging

app = Flask(__name__)

# Configure logging
#logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

@app.route('/api/checkMasterRole', methods=['GET'])
def check_master_role():
    external_api_url = "https://<SAPCC hostname>:8443/exposed?action=hasMasterRole"
    
    try:
        response = requests.get(external_api_url, verify=False)  # Set verify=True for production
        response.raise_for_status()  # Raise an error for bad HTTP responses
        
        # Log the response content for debugging
        #app.logger.debug(f"Response content: {response.content}")
        
        # Check if the response is a string representation of a boolean
        response_text = response.text.strip().lower()
        
        if response_text == 'true':
            return jsonify({"status": "healthy"}), 200  # Return 200 if master role is true
        elif response_text == 'false':
            return jsonify({"status": "unhealthy", "reason": "Not master"}), 503  # Return 503 if not master
        else:
            #app.logger.error("Response is not a recognized boolean")
            return jsonify({"status": "error", "message": "Unexpected response format"}), 500

    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as http_err:
        #app.logger.error(f"HTTP error occurred: {http_err}")
        return jsonify({"status": "unhealthy", "reason": "HTTP error"}), 503
    except Exception as e:
        #app.logger.error(f"An error occurred: {str(e)}")
        return jsonify({"status": "error", "message": "Internal Server Error"}), 500

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000)
  • Configure the probe with suitable intervals (e.g., 5 seconds) and a threshold for the number of consecutive failures (e.g., 2) to ensure quick failover. 
  • Define Load Balancing Rules: 
    • Configure a Load Balancing Rule to map incoming TCP traffic from the frontend IP to the backend pool. Set the rule to use the same port that the ABAP client RFC uses to connect to SCC 33<nr> where nr is the instance no. mentioned on the ABAP client RFC. 

       

       

  • Test and Validate the Setup: 
    • Use Azure Monitor to track metrics and validate the health probe statuses.

       

       

    • Change the ABAP client RFC connection to use the fqdn/IP of the load balancer in hostname.
    • Test the setup by accessing the service channel using the RFC connection from the ABAP client system. The TCP Load Balancer should direct traffic to the Master instance by default. 
    • Simulate a failure on the Master SCC instance to verify that the load balancer correctly routes traffic to the Shadow SCC instance.
    • sapcc1 as shadow and sapcc2 as master.
    •  

       

       

       

       

       

Reference articles- 

https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/configure-service-channel-for-rfc 

https://d.dam.sap.com/a/QJYXmLy/RTI_Communcation_Setup_Guide_v.2.5.pdf?rc=67&inline=true

3439494 - IBP RTI - Cloud Connector high availability setup 

https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/monitoring-apis 

https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/monitoring-apis#loiof6e7a7bc6af345d2a... 

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/ 

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/load-balancer-custom-probe-overview 

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/create-custom-http-health-probe-howto

entry.title: SAP EIM Information Steward Data Quality Scorecard on Power BI
entry.content:
Below are the steps to be followed to get the Data quality data in Power BI Tool:

Step 1: Create a database user and password for reporting tools to access data from Information Steward data base. (Please ensure that user and password generated for each data base reporting tool will access, such if failed data report has to be developed, user has to be created for xx_xx_FD_xx. If report has to be created for scorecard, user has to be created for data base xx_xx_LR_xx)

Step 2: Create data base Data Store in SAP Data service as shown below.

 

Step 3: Import below MMT tables from data base and design ETL based on report requirements. Tables and join you might need to setup are:

Table 1

Join type

Table 2

Join Name

Join on

MMT_Key_Data_Domain

Inner

MMT_Key_Data_Domain_Score

Join 1

KEY_DATA_DOMAIN_ID AND KEY_DATA_DOMAIN_SCORE_TYPE_CD = 'KDDR'

Join 1

Inner

MMT_Relationship

Join 2

SCORE_RULE_ID=OBJECT_ID AND CONFIGURATION_ID=CONFIGURATION_ID

Join 2

Inner

MMT_Rule

Join 3

SCORE_RULE_ID = IS_RULE_ID

Join 3

Inner

MMT_Custom_Field_Value

Join 4

SCORE_RULE_ID = OBJECT_ID

Join 4

Inner

MMT_Data_Group ( For Project)

 

PROJECT_ID = DATA_GROUP_ID

Join 4

Inner

MMT_Data_Group (For Tables)

 

REL_RELATED_OBJECT_ID = DATA_GROUP_ID

 

Step 4: To get data in table MMT_Key_Data_Domain_Score, go to CMC-> Application-> Information Steward-> Manage Utilities and schedule “Calculate Scorecard” as shown in below screenshot.

 

Step 5: Once ETL is schedule to run on set frequency, create a database user and password for the database where ETL output is saved and pass the same information to Reporting tool to setup connection and consume the scorecard data.

Step 6: Assuming report is developed in Power BI, setup an dsn using ODBC connection and enter the user and password provided.

Step 7: Table/Data is available for designing visualization based on organization requirements.

Step 8: to build report on failed data, connect reporting tool to failed data database as set in Information steward

Conclusion:

By following above steps scorecard and failed data reporting can be developed on any of the reporting tool of your organization choice although suggestion would be to use the scorecard provided in Information steward as it is user friendly and easy to use from data steward perspective.

entry.title: Enabling Access to On-Premise S/4HANA Applications in SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition: Step-by
entry.content:
Subscriptions

  • SAP Business Technology Platform
  • SAP Cloud Connector
  • SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition
  • S/4 HANA On-Premises System Setup

Pre-requisite Setup

  • Setup of SAP Business Technology Platform Account
  • Setup of SAP Cloud Connector & Trust to the SAP S/4HANA System
  • Setup of S/4HANA on-premises System
  • Setup SAP Identity and Authentication Service (optional)

Pre-requisite Checks

  1. In your BTP subaccount you are subscribed to the following subscriptions
  • Cloud Identity Services

 

  • SAP Business Application Studio (Automatically Added)

 

  • SAP Build Work Zone, Standard edition

SAP BTP Subaccount --> Instances and Subscriptions --> create

 

  • Environment – SAP Cloud Foundry Instance

 

  1. Configure Cloud Connector with S/4 HANA On-Premise System with the following details –

For the installation of the Cloud Connector please follow the steps in the SAP Help document SAP Help

You can skip installing the Cloud Connector if you’re using a demo backend system from SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL). These demo systems come with a pre-installed Cloud Connector that’s ready to use. To access it, go to the Welcome page in your CAL system, where you’ll find the relevant links. The Cloud Connector typically runs at http://<IP of your SAP CAL backend>:8443/, where you can directly connect to it.

 

  1. Check your BTP Connectivity with Cloud Connector:- login to your BTP Subaccount and go to Destinations, and select  Create Destination with the following details :

 

 

Field

Value

Name

Name of the Destination

Type

HTTP

URL

http://Virtual Host Port No.(443XX)

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User

Support User Created in On-Premise System

 

 

           

 

  1. Once the Connection is successful, test the mapping created in Cloud Connector, it should be reachable.

 

 

Execution Steps

 

Create a Site in SAP Build Work Zone

In SAP Build Work Zone, a "site" refers to a digital workspace or portal where users can access applications, content, and resources. It is the central hub for delivering a personalized and unified experience to employees, partners, or customers. Sites in SAP Build Work Zone are highly customizable and can be designed to meet specific user needs and business requirements.

  1. Login to SAP BTP Subaccount and go to instances and subscriptions, choose SAP Build Work Zone and Go to Application

 

 

In a new window, you will log in to SAP Build Work Zone Site.

Here Select Create Site.

Keep the default settings for the site same and your site is created.

Note – Make sure you have the necessary authorizations assigned to your user ID in SAP BTP. The following roles are mandatory – Launchpad_Admin, Launchpad_External_User

In SAP BTP Subaccount to Security à Usersà select Assign User Collection

 

 

Assign these roles to your User ID.

Create Destinations in SAP BTP

To display different types of app interfaces in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (like on-premise SAP S/4HANA apps and cloud-based apps), you first need to set up a way for Work Zone to access them. This is done by creating HTTP connections, called as "runtime destination, design time destination" in the SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) cockpit. This connection acts as a bridge, allowing Work Zone to securely access and display apps from both your on-premise systems and the cloud.

Creating Design-time Destination

The design-time destination defines the location from which to fetch the content that is exposed by the content provider in SAP Build Work Zone.

  1. In the SAP BTP cockpit, log on to your subaccount as administrator.
  2. Go to Connectivity --> Destinations.
  3. To configure the design-time destination, choose New Destination and enter the following information:

 

Field

Value

Name

Add the dt suffix to the name

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<VIRTUAL_HOST>:<VIRTUAL_PORT>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User

To connect to the SAP S/4HANA system, use a technical user on the destination with permission to read the CDM, for example, the technical user with the SAP_FLP_EXP_USER role, such as <SAP S/4HANA user ID> Creating System User in SAP

 

 

By choosing Additional Properties  New Property -->  sap-client: <client number>

Check the Connection for the Destination.

 

Creating Run-time Destination

The runtime destination defines the location from which to consume the content required to run the federated apps in runtime.

  1. To configure the runtime destination, choose New Destination and enter the following information:

Field

Value

Name

Add the rt suffix to the name

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<VIRTUAL_HOST>:<VIRTUAL_PORT>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User

To connect to the SAP S/4HANA system, use a technical user on the destination with permission to read the CDM, for example, the technical user with the SAP_FLP_EXP_USER role, such as <SAP S/4HANA user ID> Creating System User in SAP

 

Add Following Additional Properties to complete configuration of runtime destination.

Field

Value

HTML5.DynamicDestination

true

sap-client

Client No

sap-platform

ABAP

sap-sysid

On-Premise System ID

sap-service

32XX (XX- virtual Port)

And Check the Connection for this destination it should be successful.

 

Note - The destination name must contain only lowercase letters and numbers. Do not use the underscore (_) character.

Expose Authorization from On-Premise System

This step makes your on-premise SAP roles accessible in SAP Build Work Zone, allowing end users to access integrated Fiori applications directly within the Work Zone site.

  1. Login to SAP On-Premise system and go to T-code - /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE

Add the relevant SAP Roles you want to expose to SAP Build Work Zone.

  1. Click on Expose Content

Make sure the execution is without any errors.

Configure Site in Work Zone

  1. Log in to your SAP Build Work Zone Instance, navigate to Content Channel --> New --> Content Provider

 

  1. Add the Following Details and Save.

Note – Please specify your correct runtime and design time destinations created for build work zone.

  1.  Update the content, here the content provider should get updated without any errors.

In case you receive any errors in connection please verify your destination names , for mapping errors check connectivity with the cloud connector.

Once the content is updated you will have your roles exposed from on premise available in work zone site.

  1. Update the Content From Content Provider

Select your content provider

Add your exposed roles

Roles are now visible in content manager

  1. Go to site Directory --> Settings --> Edit

The exposed roles will be available,

Assign them to the site and Save.

  1. Add the roles in your BTP subaccount

Login to your BTP Subaccount with administrator user and go to security --> Users --> Assign Role Collections

Assign the exposed SAP roles to the user

  1. Now in your SAP Build Work Zone Site the apps are visible

entry.title: Resolving the "Enqueue Operation Without Progress" Error in SAP & Avoiding SPOF
entry.content:
SAP Issue: Enqueue Operation Without Progress Due to Max Connection Limit Breach

Introduction

In complex SAP environments, maintaining smooth communication between various components like the SAP Dispatcher, Message Server, and Enqueue Replicator is crucial for system stability. A frequent issue that arises in such scenarios is the "Enqueue Operation Without Progress" error. This typically occurs when the SAP Dispatcher loses connection to the Message Server because of a breached maximum connection limit. In this blog, we will explore this issue in detail, identify the root cause, and provide solutions to prevent such problems from occurring.

Symptoms: How to Recognize the Problem

If your SAP system is experiencing the Enqueue Operation Without Progress issue, you will notice the following errors in the Dispatcher logs:



Sat May 11 20:05:30:581 2023 *** WARNING => DpWpOperationWithoutProgress: found W303 with enqueue operation running >= 473 sec [dpxxdisp.c 4660]

 

Sat May 11 20:06:01:599 2023 DpUpdateStatusFileWith: state=YELLOW, reason=Enqueue operation without progress

 

Sat May 11 20:06:30:612 2023 *** WARNING => DpWpOperationWithoutProgress: found W294 with enqueue operation running >= 37 sec [dpxxdisp.c 4660]

 

Sat May 11 20:07:01:628 2023 DpUpdateStatusFileWith: state=YELLOW, reason=Enqueue operation without progress

 

Sat May 11 20:07:30:644 2023 *** WARNING => DpWpOperationWithoutProgress: found W294 with enqueue operation running >= 97 sec [dpxxdisp.c 4660]

 

Sat May 11 20:08:01:662 2023 DpUpdateStatusFileWith: state=YELLOW, reason=Enqueue operation without progress

 

In these logs, the recurring "Enqueue operation without progress" warning appears, indicating that the dispatcher is unable to proceed with enqueue operations, usually because of a connection issue with the Message Server. The log entries show that certain enqueue operations (such as W191 and W4) are running for extended periods, which is unusual and points to a potential network bottleneck or configuration issue.

Root Cause: Max Connection Limit Breach

The problem is often caused by the SAP system reaching its maximum connection limit, which leads to a situation where the Dispatcher cannot connect to the Message Server. This is commonly triggered by an overload of connections managed by the Internet Communication Manager (ICM) or the Enqueue Replicator.

As indicated in the logs, the system can reach its ICM connection limit, which means that the dispatcher or enqueue replicator is unable to accept new connections due to the maximum allowed connections being reached. In the log, you will see entries like:

less

 

[2023-11-03-14:20:31,983] [Thr 140356127720704] *** WARNING => IcmAcceptClient: server overload: conn: 4000 - accept delayed [icxxman.c 5779]

[2023-11-03-14:20:31,984] [Thr 140356127720704] Trigger snapshot

[2023-11-03-14:20:31,984] [Thr 140354322437888] Client IP table - not enabled or not yet initialized

 

Additionally, a Server Snapshot will indicate that the issue stems from reaching the maximum number of ICM connections, which results in delays in processing requests.

Key Configuration Parameters to Adjust

To resolve the issue and avoid it in the future, certain SAP parameters related to connections need to be optimized. These key parameters should be reviewed and adjusted:

1. enq/io/max_conn

  • Description: This parameter controls the maximum number of connections allowed for enqueue operations.

Recommendation: Ensure that enq/io/max_conn is set higher than the total number of work processes across all application servers.
Example: If you have 4 application servers and each server has 100 work processes, the total number of work processes will be 400. Therefore, enq/io/max_conn should be set to a value higher than this.


enq/io/max_conn = 10000

 

2. enq/io/max_threads

  • Description: This parameter specifies the maximum number of threads for processing connections at the enqueue server level. It controls the maximum number of threads the system can use to process incoming requests.

Recommendation: It is advisable not to set enq/io/max_threads higher than 4-6 threads based on best practices.


enq/io/max_threads = 4

  • According to SAP documentation (https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/si/3362959230.html), setting enq/io/max_threads higher than 4-6 is not recommended.

3. icm/max_conn

  • Description: This parameter specifies the maximum number of connections that the Internet Communication Manager (ICM) can handle.

Recommendation: Set icm/max_conn to a value greater than enq/io/max_conn, ideally 10,000, as a general rule of thumb.
Example:


icm/max_conn = 10000

  • This setting ensures that the ICM can handle all incoming connections without reaching the maximum limit.

Solution Approach

To resolve the issue, follow these steps:

  1. Check the current settings for the key parameters (enq/io/max_conn, icm/max_conn, and enq/io/max_threads) in your system and adjust them based on the recommendations above.
  2. Ensure that the ICM connection limit (icm/max_conn) is greater than the enq/io/max_conn value to ensure proper handling of incoming connections.
  3. Ensure the enq/io/max_threads value is set to an optimal number (e.g., 4-6 threads) to avoid overloading the Enqueue Replicator.
  4. Monitor the system after applying the changes to verify that the issue has been resolved. Use tools like ST03N or ST11 to monitor the connection behavior and ensure that the connection pool is not being exhausted.

Additional Considerations

  • Network Resources: Ensure that network resources such as bandwidth, latency, and server CPU are adequate to handle the number of connections configured. Overloading the system with connections can still lead to performance degradation even if the parameters are optimized.
  • SAP Notes and Documentation: Always refer to the latest SAP Notes and documentation for updates on configuration best practices. For example, SAP Note 2946473 provides insights into ICM connection management.

Conclusion

The Enqueue Operation Without Progress issue is typically a result of reaching the maximum connection limit in the SAP environment. By adjusting key configuration parameters like enq/io/max_conn, icm/max_conn, and enq/io/max_threads, you can resolve this issue and ensure smoother communication between SAP components. Additionally, always ensure that your network and server resources are adequate to support the number of concurrent connections required by your system.

For more detailed information and troubleshooting, check SAP's official documentation and the latest SAP Notes.

References

  • SAP Note 2946473: ICM and Enqueue Parameters
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/si/3362959230.html?q=enq/io/max_threads icm/max_conn
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/2288909/E - IcmAcceptClient: server overload
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/3196926/E -Enqueue Replicator 2 is in YELLOW status OR Enqueue Server 2 is in hung state

entry.title: How to use Derived RAP Business Events and enhance the standard SAP payload and add custom Filters
entry.content:
Introduction: In this post, we will discuss how to use Derived RAP Business Events and extend the payload that is sent to SAP Event Mesh in Event Based Architecture. We will also learn how to apply a custom Filter on the Business Event Topic with a real Business example. For starters, SAP Event Mesh is SAP’s event infrastructure solution and is a fully managed cloud service that allows applications to communicate through events.

 

What is a Derived Business Event?

Developing a RAP Business Event involves the definition of its payload and its implementation. In case you want to use an already implemented event with a different payload, you can make use of derived events. A derived event is defined with reference to an already implemented event and allows the definition of a payload that deviates from the one of the referenced events. Refer following help link to understand in detail Derived Business Events

 

Prerequisites for derived events in extensions:

If the derived event is defined in a behavior definition extension, the following prerequisites apply for the original business object:

  • The business object containing the event that is referenced by the derived event needs to be extensibility-enabled as described in RAP Extensibility-Enablement.
  • The event referenced by the derived event needs to be exposed in the C0 and C1 released RAP BO interface. If the referenced event has been added in a behavior extension, it is implicitly exposed in the RAP BO interface.
  • The CDS view entity which serves as the data source for the payload of the derived event needs to be C1 released.

 

Business scenario(example):

When a Maintenance Order is created or its status is changed in SAP S4 HANA system (using Tcode IW31 / IW32), a notification event is sent to Event Mesh with payload having only two fields (MaintenanceOrder and MaintenanceOrderType) as part of standard SAP design. Requirement here is to enhance/customize this payload and add additional fields related to Maintenance Order. Additionally add a custom Filter on the Business Event Topic to make sure only specific Order Types are sent to Event Mesh.

 

How was it solved (Step by step procedure):

Step1: First let us do the required configuration to send standard SAP notification Event for Maintenance Order. Maintenance Order Standard Business Event can be found on following Business Accelerator Hub link Maintenance Order Events. Notice the sample payload shown which has only two fields (MaintenanceOrder and MaintenanceOrderType)

 

Go to Tcode-“/IWXBE/OUTBOUND_CFG” and add the following Topic “/sap/s4/beh/maintenanceorder/v1/MaintenanceOrder/SetToInPlanning/v1” in the Outbound Binding Configuration for the Active channel which Basis Team already configured separately. The steps to create a channel is not covered in this post which is done typically by an SAP BASIS consultant. 

 

To apply filters to a Topic, we would click on filter button shown in above screenshot which takes us to below screen. But here it does not show any option to filter based on ‘Maintenance Order type’ field which we want to achieve as part of business requirement. From Step3 onwards we will see how to achieve this using Derived RAP Business Event.

 

Step2: Now that Outbound Binding Configuration is done, let us test it by creating a Maintenance Order using Tcode IW31 and see the Payload generated.

 

 

Once Maintenance Order is created as shown above, go to Tcode- “/IWXBE/EVENT_MONITOR”, choose Outbound Events radio button and provide the channel name and execute. We can see the Payload is generated by standard SAP and with only two fields.

 

Step3: Now from this step onwards we will use Derived RAP Business Events and extend the Payload with additional fields and add a custom filter.

Developing a derived Business event comprises the definition of a CDS view for the event payload and the definition of the derived event itself in a behavior definition or in a behavior definition extension.

Step3a: Create a data definition for a CDS view entity called ZMAINT_ORDER_DATASOURCE that selects from standard CDS view I_MaintenanceOrder. Add the key field “MaintenanceOrder” and add other required additional fields that we want to see in the Enhanced Payload. Add the following CDS Annotation “@Event.context.attribute: 'xsapordtype'” for the MaintenanceOrderType field, this will enable us to use this field as a Filter on the Business Event Topic in Tcode - /IWXBE/EVENT_MONITOR and /IWXBE/EVENT_FILTER.

The below created CDS view entity will provide the payload for the derived event.

@EndUserText.label: 'Test Managed / Derived Event'

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

define view entity ZMAINT_ORDER_DATASOURCE

  as select from I_MaintenanceOrder

  {

  key I_MaintenanceOrder.MaintenanceOrder,

@event.context.attribute: 'xsapordtype'

      I_MaintenanceOrder.MaintenanceOrderType,

      I_MaintenanceOrder.MaintenanceOrderDesc,

      I_MaintenanceOrder.MaintenancePlant,

      I_MaintenanceOrder.MaintenanceNotification     

} 

Step3b: Create a Behavior Extension for the standard Behavior Definition- R_MaintenanceOrderTP as shown below. For quick reference pasted below is the standard Behavior Definition in which standard Business Events are defined.

 

The resulting BDEF extension looks like this below. Right click on class zbp_r_maintenanceordertp_be and create the class.

extension implementation in class zbp_r_maintenanceordertp_be unique;

 

Add the keywords extend behavior for Order { }. The derived event can now be defined within the curly brackets.

extension implementation in class zbp_r_maintenanceordertp_be unique;

extend behavior for Order

{ 

}

Define a derived event with the name ZSetToInPlanning and with reference to the existing event SetToInPlanning. Above created CDS view entity ZMAINT_ORDER_DATASOURCE need to be passed as parameter; which will serve as the payload for the derived event.

extension implementation in class zbp_r_maintenanceordertp_be unique;

extend behavior for Order

{

   managed event ZSetToInPlanning on SetToInPlanning parameter ZMAINT_ORDER_DATASOURCE;  

  }

 

Step3c: Create an new Z-Event Binding and provide any custom Type Namespace , SAP Object Type, Operation. And then click on ADD button and provide Entity Name and Entity Event Name. Here in the Entity Event Name we need to make sure to provide the Managed/Derived Event Name(ZSETTOINPLANNING) that was declared in the Behavior Extension.

 

Step3d: Next go to Tcode-“/IWXBE/OUTBOUND_CFG” and add the following Topic “ZXXX/s4/beh/MaintenanceOrder/SetToInPlanning/v1” in the Outbound Binding Configuration for the Active channel which Basis Team already configured separately.

 

Step3e: Next click on the Filter button in above screenshot or we can go to Tcode - /IWXBE/EVENT_FILTER directly, and we see below screen. When we click on + sign button, then the newly added custom Filter appears in the list. This appears because of the CDS annotation (“@Event.context.attribute: 'xsapordtype'”) that was added above in Step3a.

We can add the custom filter now based on Maintenance Order Type as shown below. As per this Filter, it will send only Order type ‘ZABC’ related events to Event Mesh as per Business requirement mentioned in the Business Scenario section above.

 

Step4: Now that all the development and configuration steps related to Derived Business Events is completed, let us test and verify the new extended Payload, by creating a new Maintenance Order of Order type ‘ZABC’, using Tcode – IW31 as shown below.

In the below generated payload, please notice the four new additional fields related to Maintenance Order (MaintenanceOrderDesc, MaintenancePlant, MaintenanceNotification, MaintenanceNotificationText)

 

Conclusion: Using the Derived Business Events, we were able to ADD custom Filters on the Business Event Topic and we also enhanced and customized the Payload to send additional fields as per Business requirement.

entry.title: In SAP C4C-S4 integration scenario, the property owner is updated but the premise is not up back wit
entry.content:
Hi All,

In SAP C4C-S4 integration scenario, the property owner is updated but the premise is not up back with the owner.

Property owner is updated but the premise is not updated from S4 to C4C.

If somebody updates Premise manually, the premise shows the updated owner information from s4.

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Database integration with SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management for Monitoring
entry.content:
Introduction

For the businesses / projects having HANA Cloud Databases as one of the core operations, ensuring their health, performance, and availability is critical for smooth business operations. With SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management, or CALM, we can take a major step forward in how we monitor these databases, providing greater efficiency and control.

SAP CALM allows us to monitor multiple HANA Cloud Databases from one unified portal. Whether we have a single database or a larger fleet of databases, CALM gives us centralized visibility. This means that we no longer have to log into individual systems to check for performance issues or system health. From a single dashboard, we can get an instant overview of key metrics such as CPU usage, memory consumption, and disk utilization across all HANA Cloud databases.

Here is a simple guide to integrate SAP HANA Cloud DB with SAP CALM.

Step 1: Create Service Key for HANA Cloud DB

  • Log in to the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).
  • Navigate to desired subaccount within the BTP cockpit.
  • Go to the Instances and Subscriptions section.
  • Under Instances, locate the SAP HANA Cloud DB instance that needs to be integrated with SAP CALM.
  • Click on the Actions button (represented by three dots).

  • Select Create Service Key button.

  • Provide an appropriate Service Key Name for the connection and click on Create. Note that name CLOUD_KEY is an example.

  • Initially, the status Creation in Progress will be displayed under Service Keys.
  • Once done, the status of the Service Key CLOUD_KEY will be changed to Created. 

  • The newly created Service Key CLOUD_KEY can be viewed by selecting Actions button (represented by three dots) and clicking on View.

Step 2: Add SAP HANA Cloud DB in SAP CALM

  • Log in to the SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (CALM).
  • Navigate to Administration tab and select Landscape Management.

  • Inside Landscape Management, click on Select a Scope (represented by Darts symbol).

  • Under Select a Scope, find the Service Type drop down and select SAP HANA Cloud and click on Go. 

  • The Service Type SAP HANA Cloud will be filtered out. Select it and click on Apply.

  • The SAP HANA Cloud will filtered out. Click on the same to go inside.

  • Create an entry for the SAP HANA Cloud DB by clicking on Add button, then select New Service.

  • The Add Service window will appear. Please provide the necessary information and click on Save.
    • Name: Enter an appropriate name for the service.
    • System Number: Specify the system number associated with the service.
    • Service Type: Select SAP HANA Cloud as the service type.
    • Role: Indicate the role of the system (e.g., Production, Test, Development, or Demo).
    • Root URL: Enter the hostname, including the port number.
    • External ID: Provide the instance ID of the HANA Cloud DB.
  • Note that the below details are example. Valid details needs to be provided. 
    • Name: TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB
    • System Number: test_12345
    • Service Type: SAP HANA Cloud
    • Role: Demo
    • Root URL: https://XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.hana.trial-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443
    • External ID: XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX

  • Upon saving successfully, search for the Name in the Live Search box and the TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB entry with status as New will appear in the Landscape Management Page under Services.

 

  • Click on the same to see TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB specific configuration page for further actions.

Step 3: Create HTTP Endpoint of HANA Cloud DB

  • Inside the TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB configuration page, find the ENDPOINTS section and click on Add.

  • The Add Endpoint window will appear. Under General section, the Endpoint Name and Use Case field will be automatically populated with Health Monitoring. If they are not pre-filled, please enter an appropriate name and select Health Monitoring as the Use Case.
  • Under the Connectivity section, for Root URL, enter the hostname and replace the part <hostname>.hana with api.gateway.orchestration. For Example, if the hostname is "XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX.hana.trial-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com", then the Root URL will be "https://api.gateway.orchestration.trial-us10.hanacloud.ondemand.com".
  • The Authentication Type will be OAuth2ClientCredentials by default. To populate the Client ID, Client Secret, and Token Service URL, simply copy the contents of the newly created Service Key CLOUD_KEY and click the Paste Service Key button. This will automatically fill in the required fields.

  • Click on the Check Connection button to verify the Endpoint connectivity. The Endpoint Connection Check window will appear with status as Success and HTTP status code 200 OK. Post this, save the Endpoint configuration.

Step 4: Verification of Integration

  • Upon saving the Endpoint configuration, please wait for 10 to 15 minutes for the data flow.
  • The Configuration Status and the Runtime Status will show as Active and Success respectively for the Health Monitoring Use Case under SUPPORTED USE CASES section of TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB configuration page.

  • Also, the TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB entry now with status as Active will appear in the Landscape Management Page under Services.

Step 5: Monitoring of HANA Cloud DB in CALM

  • Log in to the SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (CALM).
  • Navigate to Operation tab and select Health Monitoring under Monitoring and Analysis section.

  • Inside Health Monitoring, click on Select a Scope (represented by Darts symbol).

  • In the Search, type TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB. Select the DB and click on Apply.

  • The SAP HANA Cloud will filtered out. Click on the same to go inside.

  • In the Search, type TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB such that our newly integrated SAP HANA Cloud DB TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB will be filtered out. Click on the same to get inside for more details.

  • We will be able to see the Metrics Overview of our newly integrated SAP HANA Cloud DB TEST_HANA_CLOUD_DB.

  • Click on the Setting button to open the Configuration for Services window. Here, the Threshold can be set for various Metrics under the Metrics tab and E-Mail Alert Notification can be set for the various Events under the Events tab. Note that, thresholds for system metrics depend on the type and size of the database, workloads.

entry.title: Dependency Inversion in ABAP : Practical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: Applying Design Principles in ABAP 

In ABAP development, many of us don't always focus on design principles and patterns, especially since our work often revolves around customizing existing solutions or integrating systems rather than building software from scratch. However, understanding and applying these principles can still greatly improve the quality, maintainability, and scalability of our code.

In this blog, I want to share a practical example of how we can apply the Dependency Inversion principle, a key concept in the SOLID design patterns, in our everyday ABAP tasks.

What is Dependency Inversion?

Dependency Inversion suggests that the high-level modules (which contain the core logic of the application) should not rely directly on low-level modules (which handle specific tasks or data). Instead, both should depend on abstractions.

Example Scenario: Checking the Budget

Let’s imagine that we are working on a system where we need to check the budget for a Purchase Order (PO). Here’s how the process might unfold in a typical setup without considering Dependency Inversion:

  1. Step 1: Create a PO class that has a method Check_PO_Budget().
  2. Step 2: Create a BudgetService class that interacts with the PO class.
  3. Step 3: In the BudgetService class, you instantiate a PO object and call the po_obj->Check_PO_Budget() method.

The Problem with the Initial Approach

In the example above, the BudgetService class directly depends on the PO class to check the budget. the low and high levels are tightly coupled. The BudgetService class cannot function without initiating a PO object. Another thing f you need to add new functionality, such as checking the budget for a Sales Order (SO) instead of a PO, you have to modify the BudgetService class itself .

Applying Dependency Inversion to Solve the Problem

To solve these issues, we need to decouple the high-level module (BudgetService) from the low-level module (PO). both the high and low-level modules should depend on an interface ( Abstraction layer) that defines the behavior they require, rather than depending directly on each other.

Step-by-Step Approach:

Create an Interface: Define an interface that includes the method for checking the budget.

 

 

INTERFACE zi_budge_services PUBLIC .
  METHODS :
    Check_budget RETURNING VALUE(r_out1) type string.
ENDINTERFACE.

 

 

Implement the Interface in the PO Class

 

 

CLASS zcl_po DEFINITION
    PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES zi_budge_services.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_po IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zi_budge_services~check_budget.
    r_out1 = 'Check budget for PO!' .
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

Create the High-Level Class: The BudgetService class will depend on the interface rather than the concrete PO class. 

Note that : in the instructor we a type of the Interface so that we can inject any object or class that implements the interface and use its own functionality of checking the budget . this is a technique used to apply the Dependency Inversion called dependency injection ( Constructor Injection ).

 

 

CLASS zcl_access_budget_service DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

  methods constructor
      IMPORTING !budget_service type REF TO zi_budge_services .

  methods : run_budget_services RETURNING VALUE(r_output) type string .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
      data : budget_service type REF TO zi_budge_services .
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zcl_access_budget_service IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    me->budget_service = budget_service .
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD run_budget_services.
       data(o1) = budget_service->check_budget(  ) .
       r_output = o1 .
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

 

Test the logic:

 

 

CLASS ztest_di DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS ztest_di IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.

     DATA(lo_po) = NEW zcl_po(  ) .
     data(lo_budget1) =  new zcl_access_budget_service( lo_po ) .
     data(output)     = lo_budget1->run_budget_services( ) .

  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

Introducing New Functionalities Without Modifying High-Level Modules : The high level BudgetService  remains untouched. We will add a new budget checker (SO) then just Inject it into the BudgetService .

 

 

CLASS zcl_so DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zi_budge_services.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_so IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zi_budge_services~check_budget.
     r_out1 = 'Check budget for SO!' .
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

Nothing to be changed in the BudgetService  main Class and only we Inject the SO Object in the testing class like below 

 

 

    DATA(lo_so) = NEW zcl_so(  ) .

    data(lo_budget1) =  new zcl_access_budget_service( lo_so ) .
    data(output)     = lo_budget1->run_budget_services( ) .

 

 

 

Conclusion

In the blog I tried to highlight the benefits of Dependency Inversion with Dependency Injection as below :

 

  • Decoupling: The high-level BudgetService class is now independent of the low-level PO and SO classes. This makes it easier to change or replace them without affecting the core logic.
  • Flexibility: New types of budget checkers (like PO_V2 or SO) can be introduced with minimal impact on the existing system.
  • Easier Maintenance: Changes in the low-level classes (e.g., the PO class) do not require changes in the high-level module (e.g., the BudgetService class), reducing the risk of introducing bugs.

This is my first time to work with this pattern and I wanted to share what I have learned , So feel free to try it out in your projects and let me know your thoughts.

entry.title: Benefits of SAP MDG Solution
entry.content:
Benefits of SAP MDG Solution

Here are the key benefits of SAP's Master Data Governance (MDG) solution

  • SAP MDG performs effective master data management. It helps to improve data quality, consistency, a

     

    nd accuracy. Many industries use SAP MDG to increase customer experience, reduce costs, enhance productivity, and speed up new product launches.
  • Flexibility is one of the key benefits of SAP MDG. SAP MDG allows customization. It is a centralized platform that helps manage master data across various domains such as HRM, ERP, CRM, and supply chain.
  • SAP MDG supports managing both structured and unstructured data. So you can manage customer and product data, emails, and so on.

Central Governance

You can create and maintain master data with SAP MDG. You can streamline master data using a central system that adheres to data rules and standards. With this approach, master data is maintained in a central system and then replicated in satellite systems. 

It ensures that the downstream system transactions leverage master data, as shown in the below image.

Know that SAP MDG manages the master data through a clear audit trail. It provides significant business benefits, improving business operations.

SAP MDG helps to avoid error-prone manual maintenance on master data. It offers centralized governance and data replication to systems. Moreover, it ensures data consistency and maps master data across entire enterprise systems.

 

Consolidation for Analytics Purpose Only

Master data is usually created in standalone or transactional systems based on needs. You must consolidate and centralize the master data for data analytics. It can be done by merging and mapping the data to standard data. The image below explains the same.

It enables organizations to easily manage and maintain a single version of the truth for all their critical data. 

This approach allows organizations to gain valuable insights and make informed decisions with accurate and reliable data.

 

 

Consolidation for Initial Load Before Central Governance

It is necessary to merge and create the best record to consolidate master data in the central governance scenario. You can achieve the same by using the consolidation functionalities of SAP MDG. It is usually a one-time activity in which companies manage their master data and replicate it into multiple systems.

The consolidation scenario helps to extract, cleanse, and consolidate data during the initial load. It helps prepare the existing data for central governance, ensuring it is accurate reliable, and adheres to data rules and standards. 

With SAP MDG, organizations can streamline this process, enabling them to easily manage and maintain their master data and replicate it across multiple systems.

 

Consolidation for Mergers or Acquisitions

Introducing new systems into an enterprise's landscape can create data harmonization and duplication challenges.

However, companies can synchronize and de-duplicate new data with the existing data using the consolidation functionalities of SAP MDG. Using SAP MDG, enterprises can merge and map data to a common data standard, ensuring that new data is consistent and accurate with the existing data. This helps avoid the creation of duplicate records, ensuring that the data remains clean and reliable. 

By using SAP MDG's consolidation functionalities, organizations can streamline harmonizing and de-duplicating new data. It enables organizations to quickly integrate new systems and leverage the full potential of their master data.

Continuous Hybrid Approach

If you want to refine master data from source systems, you must combine the consolidation and central governance scenarios. It helps to manage the enhanced data using a significant governance process. 

SAP Master Data Governance offers several functionalities to support various use cases, including:

  • Business-driven workflow and workflow rules framework to automate master data management
  • Reducing implementation costs by reusing data validation as well as mapping logic.
  • Integrate with SAP data model for key master data domains
  • Customize master data objects' configuration frameworks to optimize data management.
  • Provide extension capabilities for standard master data objects
  • Provide APIs and integration capabilities to access data and use change logs.
  • monitoring data management processes for built-in master data stewardship tools
  • managing data quality through business rules repository
  • analyzing data quality for product and master records
  • correcting data quality issues through worklists and embedded analytics

 

 

Differences Between SAP MDG and SAP MDM

Here are the key differences between MDM and MDG:   

Criteria

MDM

MDG

Definition

It ensures the quality, consistency, and accuracy of data across the enterprise

It provides a way to manage master data in multiple systems while maintaining consistency

Data Storage

You can store data in one central location

You can store data in multiple systems

Data Distribution

Data is distributed from the central location to other systems

Data is replicated across multiple systems

Single Version of Truth

Provides a single version of truth for data

Provides consistency for data stored in multiple systems

Use Case

Useful when you need to synchronize data across the organization

Useful when you need to maintain data consistency across multiple systems

 

 

                                                                                                                                            Published By

                                                                                                                                            Nikhat Fatima

entry.title: The easiest way to detect background jobs with locked/invalid user and prevent the terminated jobs.
entry.content:
Well, I wrote this blog post because I faced a lot of problems with terminated background jobs due to the step user is invalid or locked. Then I question myself, how can I determine those jobs earlier and take preventive action before they’re terminated?

Luckily, I found a very useful report which was mentioned in SAP Note 1533612. Although that note is pretty old (last update was on 2018), but we can still use it on S/4HANA. Nice!!

You can execute that report with SE38/SA38 at your choice. At the main screen, you will have the option to investigate the released/scheduled or terminated jobs and see whether the step user is deleted, locked or invalid. In my case, I don’t want to check the terminated jobs because they happened already. Therefore, I chose the “Released” jobs only. And the action is only Display because I want to check them first. You can also decide to Cancel or Delete those jobs as you wish.

As a result, I found 5 background jobs, which will be terminated in the future because the step users are invalid. How nice and easy it is!

Then, what I’ll do, is change the Step user to a valid one. But I don’t want to do it one by one. Instead, we can use report BTC_MASS_JOB_CHANGE, which is also a very nice tool to change many backgrounds at the same time.

I hope that this blog post will help all my beloved SAP Basis to prevent the terminated jobs situation which is sometime very stressful. Thank you and happy learning!!

entry.title: Handling SAP On-Prem Financials with IBM Apptio
entry.content:
Simplifying SAP On-Prem Financials with Clarity, Efficiency, and Insights

Managing SAP on-premise financials can be complex, especially when addressing areas like vendor management, IT tower management, and IT services management. With IBM Apptio, organizations can simplify these processes through seamless data integration, enhanced visibility, and automated financial analysis. This blog covers how IBM Apptio’s integration with SAP on-premise systems can streamline these critical financial functions, using real-world use cases to illustrate the impact.

Value Proposition

IBM Apptio’s Datalink integrates with SAP on-premise systems, offering enhanced visibility into cost management and financial performance. By automating data collection and providing powerful reporting capabilities through TBM Studio, Apptio enables organizations to make better-informed financial decisions while reducing manual effort in financial management.

  • Streamlined Financial Data Aggregation and Reporting: Apptio Datalink efficiently gathers and reports SAP financial data, reducing manual effort and time.
  • Enhanced Cost Visibility: Integration with SAP financials enables Apptio to provide actionable insights, helping stakeholders understand and manage costs more effectively.
  • Targeted Data Mapping in TBM Studio: With data visualization capabilities in TBM Studio, users can map financial data schemas to specific organizational needs, improving analysis accuracy.

Architecture Overview

 

The integration between SAP on-premise systems and IBM Apptio comprises several key components:

  • Apptio Frontdoor > Datalink: Acts as the gateway for Apptio’s data collection.

                                  

 

  • Datalink Agents: Fetch and transmit SAP cost data, securing access through tokens and proxies.

              

 

  • Datalink Connections: Support seamless data flow and integration with SAP financial sources.

 

Each component plays a vital role in capturing and managing financial data for streamlined reporting and analysis.

IBM Apptio Offerings

IBM Apptio’s offerings are designed to address these challenges. Datalink sits within the SAP on-premise systems, acting as a powerful data bridge that works inside or outside firewall constraints. Key functionalities include:

  • Datalink Agent: Positioned within SAP environments, Datalink gathers cost reports seamlessly and provides a central repository for data aggregation.
  • Authentication Tokens and Proxies: Securely establish connections to SAP data sources, enabling easy access to essential financial metrics.
  • Streamlined Data Management: Apptio consolidates and organizes data from SAP financials, making it accessible for advanced financial analysis and management in TBM Studio.

Planning, Staging, and Reporting in TBM Studio

IBM Apptio’s TBM Studio offers a robust platform for planning and staging reports before they go live. The process includes:

  1. Report Development: Building and testing reports to capture essential SAP financials.
  2. Error Resolution: Correcting any issues before final deployment.
  3. Production Release: Moving reports from development to staging and, finally, to production.

Use Cases

IBM Apptio’s integration with SAP on-premise financials provides valuable insights and streamlined management for key financial areas such as:

  • Vendor Management: Tracking and analyzing vendor-related costs to improve budgeting and alignment with business goals.
  • IT Tower Management: Centralizing and optimizing costs for IT resources like networking, storage, and compute.
  • IT Services Management: Managing the financials of IT services to ensure efficient budgeting and planning.

Conclusion

Managing SAP on-premise financials doesn’t have to feel like solving a giant jigsaw puzzle. With IBM Apptio, the pieces start to fall into place, giving you a clear picture of your financials.

  • See the Bigger Picture: Gain real-time insights into your financial data, bringing much-needed clarity and transparency to the table.
  • Say Goodbye to Manual Hassles: Automate the heavy lifting of cost aggregation, freeing up time for smarter budgeting and planning.
  • Turn Data into Decisions: Use TBM Studio to uncover actionable insights that help you make confident financial choices.

By integrating IBM Apptio with SAP on-prem systems, you can demystify financial management—whether it’s tracking vendor expenses, optimizing IT tower costs, or managing IT services. It’s not just about numbers anymore; it’s about turning those numbers into strategies that make sense for your business.

entry.title: Re: 4.3 Web Intelligence Data Lineage Challenge
entry.content:
Thanks for the response Christian.

I would challenge the assumption that the universe objects from 2 different queries albeit from the same universe are the same and should be renamed in this manner.

It effectively assumes that you are joining on all the dimensions from both queries, but what if you are only joining on one dimension in the query because you want compare the values in the other dimensions (joining in data mode is some ways similar to merging dimensions, but obviously more powerful)

So 2 queries with the same dimensions X,Y and you choose to join only on X because you want to compare the values being stored in dimension Y in both queries.

I only started to think about renaming the objects in the original queries because you cannot identify the lineage of the objects from their original queries. I ended up renaming the objects in the cube, but it's a tricky exercise. Tooltip is a start, but perhaps colorising by data source would be clearer

 

thanks again for engaging

 

James

entry.title: Re: CALM & MS Teams Integration changes due to MS O365 co...
entry.content:
Hi,

On the contrary, the configuration in your second image ("USING WORKFLOWS") is actually the same whether you were previously using MS Teams Incoming Webhooks or are now using MS Teams Workflows. 

The MS Teams documentation Create an Incoming Webhook illustrates that the Incoming Webhooks are created per-channel, not per Team or per Installation. I can't find any evidence that MS Teams Incoming Webhooks can be shared across different Teams or Channels. So this means that you already needed to create individual BTP destinations for each MS Teams Channel that was using an MS Teams Incoming Webhook. 

Here is a graphic that represents the hierarchy and structure a bit clearer. It incorporates both MS Teams Incoming Webhooks and MS Teams Workflows.

SAP Cloud ALM already supports the newer MS Teams Workflows. They have to be maintained in much the same manner as the older MS Teams Incoming Webhooks. In other words, each MS Teams Workflow needs its own BTP destination, the same way that each MS Teams Incoming Webhook needed its own BTP destination. Then a "webhook" must be created in the External API Management "Webhooks management" screen. 

The Devs have no plans to add a specific "Workflows Management" screen in the SAP Cloud ALM External API Management app. The name "Webhooks Management" in the External API Management app won't change either. This is because "webhook" in the SAP Cloud ALM context describes a general concept (a connection between an "External API Management" subscription and a BTP destination for an external API), and not a specific technology (MS Teams Incoming Webhooks). 

I hope that this clarifies this point for you.

Thanks and regards, 
John

entry.title: Encryption using a public key with function SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE
entry.content:
Hi,

We need to encrypt a KEY, like 

<KEY>290B0B57A7C7AD3D9DF170511C1C9D209DAB1C7F7675D370</KEY>

For this we need to use a public key using algorithm PKCS v1.2., to get a key of 512 hexadecimal characters.

We are trying the following:

* We have defined a new SSF application.

* We have used transaction STRUST to upload the certificate that contains the public key to be used for the encryption.

* We have configured the SSF parameters for this new application:

           HASHALG ==> SHA512

           ENCRALG ==> TRIPLE-DES

* We use functions:

     ==> SSF_GET_PARAMETER(Read certificates included in SSFA)

     ==> SSFC_GET_CERTIFICATELIS (to get  the information of each certificate)

     ==> SSFC_PARSE_CERTIFICATE 

     ==> SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE (to encrypt), here we complete:

ostr_input_data with the string we want to encrypt:<KEY>290B0B57A7C7AD3D9DF170511C1C9D209DAB1C7F7675D370</KEY> 

we are not sure wich value we need to populte field  ostr_input_data_l .Based on method EXTERNAL_ENCYPTING of class CL_PCA_SECURITY, we are applying the same logic:

* preparations
    lv_length         = strlen( iv_card_number ).
* Unicode or Non-unicode systems
    lv_length         = lv_length * lv_bytes.

So in our case we are getting: ostr_input_data_l = 118

The function SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE returns ostr_enveloped_data, we have try to apply different conversions to this encrypted data but we get 510 characters instead of 512, so we not sure if we are missing something.

entry.title: Spend Connect Live, liderazgo SAP en Procurement y otros temas
entry.content:
Un nuevo blog de JEFF COLLIER (SAP ISBN CRO ) se publicó por esos días ! SAP Spend Connect Live Hits the Innovation Jackpot. No solo destaca el éxito de SAP Spend Connect Live y las innovaciones presentadas allí (hay un link con más detalles que está imperdible de Manoj  Swaminathan - ISBN Products Leaser)  sino que también celebra el liderazgo de mercado de SAP (IDC), los premios de satisfacción de clientes y sus implementaciones exitosas de los clientes, mientras agradece a los clientes por su colaboración y espera con ansias futuros desarrollos.

Está super interesante, vale mucho la pena.

(Si tu navegador permite, haz click con e boton derecho y traduz a tu idioma).

entry.title: Net value is 0 in settlement document SD
entry.content:
Hello Community,

I want to mention a problem that I faced. 

I'm trying to create settlement document with WB2R_SC. When I create the document net and gross value does not update. I can see prices and conditions in item details but net and gross value is zero. How can i solve this problem? 

I'm waiting for your solution advises.
Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Audit Trail Requirements
entry.content: This is an informative article, Srinath. It clarifies the confusion between books of account and books of paper. It would be good to add the SAP recommendations for database audit policies for the private and the public cloud.

entry.title: Re: Using the UI5 Mockserver for Client-Specific Data and Testing
entry.content:
Thanks, Marian, for this great post! It's sometimes really helpful when working with customers who use VMs but don't have the right settings for testing. In such cases, using Mock data saves a lot of time during development. It’s also incredibly useful for running automated e2e tests in the pipeline, especially with wdi5 tests integrated with the MockServer. Additionally, since we work with different customers its good solution to use different mock data, make things testable. 
Great job highlighting the potential of this tool!

entry.title: Digital Manufacturing - Production Process Monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hi all,
in this blog, I would like to share a sample how to setup and use Job & Automation Monitoring in SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM) to monitor Digital Manufacturing Production Processes centrally.
This capability will be available with the Digital Manufacturing 2411 release.

If you are not yet aware about SAP Cloud ALM, I encourage you to start with the learning journey for Introducing SAP Cloud ALM or to take a look on the overview page.

Introduction

SAP Cloud ALM Job and Automation Monitoring provides a central job monitoring solution for all SAP products to complement local job scheduling. It gives you transparency about the status of the different automation processes (called “jobs” for simplification) regarding execution status, application status, start delay, and run time. 

With the SAP Digital Manufacturing 2411 release, we enabled the Job & Automation Monitoring functionality in SAP Cloud ALM to support the new job type SAP DM Production Process.

This allows you to monitor SAP Digital Manufacturing production processes in one main place, including the following SAP Cloud ALM capabilities:

  • Centrally monitor the health of job executions and detect anomalies during execution,
    including direct navigation to the local production process monitoring in SAP Digital Manufacturing.
  • Promptly alert both business and IT users about disruptions and exceptions.
    You can configure events in SAP Cloud ALM to inform stakeholders who need to react to exceptions.
  • Use the analysis view to gain insights on historical execution information.
    You can use the analysis view to identify the jobs with the longest response times and highest number of exceptions. A trend icon indicates if the failure rate or response time is up or down in the selected time frame, taking the midpoint as the comparison point.

Note: Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM shouldn’t replace the local SAP Digital Manufacturing Monitor Production Processes app but rather complement it to support monitoring and collaboration between different stakeholders.

Prerequisites

  • You’ve SAP CLOUD ALM provisioned.
    If you don’t have an SAP Cloud ALM tenant yet, check out the SAP Cloud ALM support page, where you can find all the required information.
    Note: SAP Cloud ALM is part of SAP Enterprise Support. The usage rights are included in the SAP Cloud Service subscription and in SAP Enterprise Support. Details on usage rights please check out Usage Rights.
  • You’ve completed the user authorization setup.
  • The SAP Digital Manufacturing Service is available in SAP Cloud ALM Landscape Management. This should happen automatically.
    Cloud services that you subscribe to are automatically imported and updated from SAP's Cloud Landscape Directory. The initial import contains all cloud services that belong to the same customer number as SAP Cloud ALM. You can maintain a Corporate Group (CCC) S-User to also import cloud services that belong to subsidiaries of the same corporate group. For more information, see Landscape Setup.  

SAP Cloud ALM Setup & Configuration

Select Your Scope for Job & Automation Monitoring

To see your SAP Digital Manufacturing service in SAP Cloud ALM, you first need to make sure you’ve set the scope accordingly. Open your SAP Cloud ALM application and navigate to Operations -> Job & Automation Monitoring
choose Scope.

In the Select a Scope popup, search for Service Type Digital Manufacturing, which will bring up your tenants.

Configuration

For general configuration of SAP Cloud ALM Job & Automation Monitoring, see  https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/jm-configuration

Choose the configuration wheel to open the configuration panel.

Managed Components

Switch on Data Collection for Job & Automation Monitoring

You can directly switch on the data collection using the toggle switch, or you can choose the name, and a detailed configuration popup will be displayed. Once you switch on the data collection for the system all new production process executions will be pushed to SAP Cloud ALM and visible in the central monitor.

Note: Only production process instances that were running at the point in time when you enabled data collection or are started later will be transferred. No historical data will be pushed to SAP Cloud ALM.
Production Process will send instances to SAP Cloud ALM every 10 mins with the data generated in the last 10 mins.

If you select a system, you can also add events to enable alerting. For more information, see in the Alerting chapter. 
For more information on managed components, application settings, and monitoring rating rules, see  https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/jm-configuration.

Note: High run times are only considered as yellow or red if the run time is longer than 1 minute. For SAP Digital Manufacturing processes, this setting might be too long.

Capabilities

Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM

Overview – The overview provides a summary status of production process executions. It shows the status of the latest execution of every job with regards to the technical application exceptions or the run time. It also displays the number of unconfirmed alerts and status events. 

Choosing a card takes you to the monitoring view, which shows the list of production processes grouped by the production process design with their latest execution status, application status, start delay, and runtime. In this view, we still see an aggregation of the instances processed in the selected time frame. 

Note: In the current version, we don’t differentiate between technical exceptions and application exceptions. The mapping between SAP Digital Manufacturing statuses and SAP Cloud ALM statuses is as follows:

DM StatusCALM Execution StatusCALM Application Status
RunningGreyGrey
FailedRedGrey
Completed with ErrorGreenGrey
CompletedGreenGrey
TerminatedYellowGrey

You can filter for a specific production process design, for example ResourceStatusChange. 

By choosing the production process design name, you can further drill down to the list of executions of the selected production process design. In the example below, 216 calls of the production process ResourceStatusChange / 1.4 have been executed. 7 instances failed and one call shows an exceptional long runtime of app. 10 sec.

Additionally, choosing the information icon provides further details and enables direct navigation to the job within the managed component by selecting the Run ID.

Further drill-down on instance level  will also show you the different steps executed in the production process execution. 

Alerting

You can configure alerts using the configuration dialog. Open the configuration dialog by choosing the configuration wheel.

Choose the component for which you would like to set the configuration.

Choose the service name. Now you can create events.

Event Settings

In the context of SAP Digital Manufacturing, we support the following cases:

  • Critical Application / Execution Status
    Note: Check details on SAP Digital Manufacturing status mapping.
  • Critical Runtime

Critical delay isn’t applicable for type SAP DM Production Processes as of today. 

Raise Event Action
By setting the Raise Event Action, you can define how you would like to get informed.  "At new occurrence" means when there’s a change to the event rating (to red or yellow). As a consequence, no new notification is sent on consecutive failures of the same job.

If you need a new notification at every job execution failure, you can configure to get the event action triggered "At every occurrence".

As SAP Digital Manufacturing runs the PPD for multiple plants with different parameters, which can cause different behavior for the different production process executions, it’s recommended to set the Raise Event Action to “At every occurrence”.

Event Filters
Within the configuration, you can set Event Filters, which means you can specify alerts for specific automation jobs (production process designs) or for specific steps / executables (production process steps).

Limitation:
At the moment, we don’t pass information from SAP Digital Manufacturing to SAP Cloud ALM for the following parameters:

  • Step Executed by
  • Job / Automation Scheduled by

Therefore, no filter options are possible for those parameters for the SAP DM Production Processes type. Depending on your configuration, you’ll receive an information e.g. via an Alert or Email.

Alerts are raised per job and alert type and contain all failed executions of a specific job.
Note: The alert has the status closed as the last execution was successful.

Analysis 

The Analysis view allows users to pinpoint jobs with extended response times and highest number of exceptions. For any job it shows aggregated information on total and average run time, number of executions and number of failed executions and the resulting failure rate.
In the example below we can see that ResourceStatusChange / 1.4 has the highest number of failed instances, however the previous version 1.3 shows a long runtime.
By clicking on the arrow at the end of every line, we can drill down into various trend charts for the metrics. 

SAP Cloud ALM Analytics API

In case you would like to store and build your own dashboards please check out the SAP Cloud ALM Analytics API. They will enable you to build dashboards and reports aggregating all types of data managed by SAP Cloud ALM for operations.

For details check out: https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/calm-apis-for-operations.html

Conclusion

In this blog post, you learned how to enable and configure the Job & Automation Monitor to in Cloud ALM for Production Processes to incorporating them into your overall landscape monitoring for efficient operation of the end-to-end solution.

Do you like this post? Please let me know what you think in the comments section.  Any feedback is greatly appreciated. If you have any questions, please visit the SAP Community Q&A Area or comment down below.  And last but not least some additional links to useful information.

  • SAP Cloud ALM for Operations Expert Portal
    https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal.html
  • Blog: Job and Automation Monitoring features - SAP Cloud ALM https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/job-and-automation-monitoring-features-sap-cloud-alm/ba-p/13643600
  • Blog: Digital Manufacturing - Integration Message Monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM
    https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-sap/digital-manufacturing-integration-message-monitoring-with-sap-cloud-alm/ba-p/13751158

entry.title: digital skills initiative
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I hope this message finds you well. My name is ahmed youssef and I am truly excited about the opportunity to join the Digital Skills Initiative. i have been working in linux-plus in egypt as sap sd consultant for the past 2 years. Throughout my career, I've recognized the immense value of staying up-to-date with digital advancements and expanding my skill set in this fast-paced world. sharpening my technical expertise and staying competitive in the evolving digital landscape.I am confident that this initiative will not only enhance my current capabilities but also empower me to contribute more effectively to the digital transformation efforts within my organisation and beyond.

Thank you for considering my application, and I look forward to the possibility of joining this incredible learning journey. Please let me know how I can proceed with the registration process.

Best regards,

@David_Chaviano 

#SD
